summary: Stellar California Earthquake and tight Bertha; second set flows.
blurb: The show kicks off with a punchy California Earthquake before Jerry launches into soaring licks on Feel Like A Stranger. Bertha rises as the high point of the first set and the second set delivers a seamless Terrapin Station jam, stellar drumming, and Brent’s fiery vocals on We Can Run.
review: On October 23 1989 at Charlotte Coliseum the Dead delivered a show that feels like a late‑’80s high water mark. A thunderous "California Earthquake (Whole lotta Shakin' Goin' On)" opens the night, and Jerry Garcia immediately stakes his claim with “exceptional licks” and the kind of “Jerrycizing” fans love. The first set builds through a soulful "Feel Like A Stranger" into a standout "Bertha," where Phil’s bass “drives the bus” and Mickey & Bill lock in a tight, poly‑rhythmic groove that carries the band forward. The second set is a flowing masterpiece: a seamless segue from "Touch of Grey" to "Looks Like Rain," a wild "Crazy Fingers > Terrapin Station" stretch, and a soaring drum/space excursion that showcases what reviewers called “insane drumming.” Brent Mydland adds vocal fire on "We Can Run" and a shredding solo on "Walkin' Blues," while the band wraps it all up with an uplifting "Turn On Your Lovelight" and a tender encore of "Attics Of My Life." The performance is consistently praised across recordings, making this one of the more memorable shows from the era.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Five of seven recording analyses are positive, highlighting exceptional guitar work, tight rhythm section, standout songs (California Earthquake, Bertha, Terrapin Station) and superb drumming. No consistent negative comments appear, so a strong positive sentiment adjustment is applied.

best_recording.reason: Crisp soundboard mix with solid balance, minimal hiss, and the clearest capture of the night’s highlights—especially California Earthquake and Bertha.

key_highlights: Killer "California Earthquake (Whole lotta Shakin' Goin' On)" that launches the show.
"Bertha" erupts as the first‑set high point with tight rhythm section interplay.
Second‑set flow shines in the seamless "Crazy Fingers > Terrapin Station" jam and insane drumming from Mickey & Bill.
Brent Mydland’s vocal fire on "We Can Run" and his shredding solo on "Walkin' Blues".
song_highlights: California Earthquake (Whole lotta Shakin' Goin' On)

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers exceptional licks and “Jerrycizing” throughout, adds memorable midi‑horn riffs on Let It Grow, and locks in tight interplay with Bob on Tennessee Jed.
band_performance.Phil: Provides a solid, driving bass foundation that propels the band, noted as "driving the bus" despite occasional mix muddiness in some recordings.
band_performance.Bob: Shows tight jam chemistry with Jerry, especially highlighted during Tennessee Jed.
band_performance.Brent: Vocal fire shines on We Can Run; his keyboards sit well in the mix and he delivers a shredding solo on Walkin' Blues.
band_performance.Mickey: Displays powerful, poly‑rhythmic groove; drumming praised as “insane” and integral to the second‑set flow.
band_performance.Billy: Pairs with Mickey for tight, dynamic percussion that underpins the jam sections and fuels the band's energy.

must_listen_sequences: California Earthquake (Whole lotta Shakin' Goin' On)
Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad
